coredata.dc:title: Fluorometric and electrochemical dual-mode detection of toxic flavonoid rutin based on new nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots: Enhanced selectivity based on masking the interfering flavonoids with BSA complexation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
Mahnashi, Mater H.
Al Fatease, Adel
Mostafa, Mahmoud A.H.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Ali, Ramadan
coredata.dc:description: A simple, cost-effective, and convenient bimodal strategy for the detection of toxic flavonoid rutin was proposed. The strategy depends on fluorometric and electrochemical determination of rutin using new type of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S@C-dots). The fluorescence detection based on quenching the emission of N, S@C-dots by rutin through inner-filter effect (IFE), while the electrochemical detection based on direct oxidation of rutin at glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with N, S@C-dots. Many factors affecting fluorometric and electrochemical measurements were optimized. The decrease of emission intensity and the increase of the electrochemical signal are linear over the concentration range of 0.02−92.3 μM and 0.2−130 × 10−8 M, respectively. The limits of detections (LODs) were found to be 8.0 nM and 0.8 nM for the fluorometric and electrochemical methods, respectively. Moreover, short response times (2.0 and 2.5 min) were achieved using the fluorometric and electrochemical methods, respectively. The selectivity of the fluorometric sensor towards rutin was enhanced; in the presence of other interfering flavonoids; by the addition of bovine serum albumin (BSA).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rutin
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots
Fluorometric detection
Electrochemical detection
Short response times
Selectivity
Masking interferences
Bovine serum albumin

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S@C-dots)
Fluorescence and electrochemical sensing of rutin
Fluorescence and electrochemical sensing mechanism of rutin
Comparison with other reported fluorometric and electrochemical sensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) particle size distribution, (c) Absorption and fluorescence spectra, (d) FTIR, (e) PXRD, and (f) Raman spectrum of the as-synthesized N, S@C-dots.
The effect of potential scan rate on the electro-chemical oxidation of 5.0 μM rutin at of N, S@C-dots/GCE. Conditions of CV are pH = 7.0 (B.R. buffer) and accumulation time of 100 s.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, S@C-dots after addition of different concentrations of rutin (0:0, 1: 0.15, 2: 0.3, 3: 0.6, 4: 1.2, 5: 1.5, 6: 3.1, 7: 6.1, 8: 9.2, 9: 13.9, 10: 18.5, 11: 23.1, 12: 30.8, 13: 38.5, 14: 46.2, 15: 53.8, and 16: 92.3 μM). Conditions are pH and incubation time of 7.0 and 2.0 min, respectively. (B) The corresponding calibration plot. (C) The effect of successive addition of rutin at N, S@C-dots/GCE (0: blank, 1:2.0, 2:5.0, 3:10.0, 4: 20.0, 5:30.0, 6:40.0, 7: 55.0, 8:70.0, 9: 85.0, 10:100.0, 11: 115.0, and 12: 130.0 × 10−8 M). Conditions of DPV are pH = 7.0 (B.R. buffer), accumulation potential of +0.1 V, accumulation time of 150 s, pulse height of 40 mV, pulse width of 0.045 s and step height of 12 mV. (D) The corresponding calibration plot.

(A) Effects of coexisting substances (50.0 μM) on the determination of rutin (5.4 μM). Conditions are pH = 7, incubation time = 2.0 min, 5.0 μL of 20.0 μM BSA was used for the selectivity study in the presence of flavonols. (B) The effect of 50.0 μM interfering species on the electrochemical oxidation of 25.0 × 10−8 M rutin at N, S@C-dots/GCE. Conditions of DPV are pH = 7.0 (B.R. buffer), accumulation potential of +0.1 V, accumulation time of 150 s, pulse height of 40 mV, pulse width of 0.045 s and step height of 12 mV.

(A) and (B) are UV/VIS absorption spectra of rutin and quercetin in absence and presence of 5.0 μL of 20.0 μM BSA, respectively while (C) and (D) are the fluorescence spectra of N, S@C-dots in the absence and presence of 5.0 μL of 20.0 μM BSA, respectively.
Preparation of N, S@C-dots and its utility for the fluorometric and the electrochemical determination of rutin.
The proposed electrochemical oxidation of rutin.
Determination of rutin using N, S@C-dots sensor by the fluorometric method in tablets, serum, and orange samples (n=4).
Determination of rutin using N, S@C-dots/GCE sensor by electrochemical method in tablets, serum, and orange samples (n=4).
Comparison between the N, S@C-dots bimodal sensor and other sensors for the rutin determination.

row.entry: Functionalized gold organosulfur self-assembled monolayer with conducting polymer and carbon nanotubes
Single-walled carbon nanotubes modified gold electrode

head.ce:title: Fluorometric and electrochemical dual-mode detection of toxic flavonoid rutin based on new nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots: Enhanced selectivity based on masking the interfering flavonoids with BSA complexation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, cost-effective, and convenient bimodal strategy for the detection of toxic flavonoid rutin was proposed. The strategy depends on fluorometric and electrochemical determination of rutin using new type of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S@C-dots). The fluorescence detection based on quenching the emission of N, S@C-dots by rutin through inner-filter effect (IFE), while the electrochemical detection based on direct oxidation of rutin at glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with N, S@C-dots. Many factors affecting fluorometric and electrochemical measurements were optimized. The decrease of emission intensity and the increase of the electrochemical signal are linear over the concentration range of 0.02−92.3 μM and 0.2−130 × 10−8 M, respectively. The limits of detections (LODs) were found to be 8.0 nM and 0.8 nM for the fluorometric and electrochemical methods, respectively. Moreover, short response times (2.0 and 2.5 min) were achieved using the fluorometric and electrochemical methods, respectively. The selectivity of the fluorometric sensor towards rutin was enhanced; in the presence of other interfering flavonoids; by the addition of bovine serum albumin (BSA).

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Rutin, 3′, 4′, 5, 7-tetrahydroxyflavone-3-d-rutinoside, is extensively used for the treatment of several diseases due to its anti-inflammatory, anti-cancer, anti-diabetic, anti-adipogenic, anti- hemorrhagic, anti-aging, and anti-oxidant properties (Chua, 2013; Hasumura et al., 2004). Excessive intake of rutin leads to some hazardous effects such as headache, rashes, nervousness, blurred vision, and change of heartbeat rate (Ganeshpurkar and Saluja, 2017). Thus, it is essential to develop validated analytical methodologies to analyze rutin in different matrices. Current approaches for rutin quantitation include HPLC (Santagati et al., 2008) and capillary zone electrophoresis (Wang et al., 2012a). However, these techniques are costly and require highly skilled persons and complicated equipment, thereby rendering them impractical in developing regions (Alyami et al., 2021; Ali et al., 2020a,b). To address these disadvantages, many ultrasensitive and low-cost analytical approaches have been developed to detect rutin based on spectrophotometric, fluorescent, and electrochemical methods (Xu et al., 2010; Liu, 2013; Liu et al., 2017), which have overcome the problems related to traditional analytical methods. Notably, fluorescent and electrochemical sensors have been widely used for sample analysis owing to their reliability and sensitivity. For example, Liu et al. developed SH-β-cyclodextrin functionalized graphene-palladium nanoparticles for the electrochemical detection of rutin with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3 × 10−10 mol L−1 (Liu et al., 2017). Moreover, Li et al. established tiny carbon dots with high quantum yield using multi-walled carbon nanotubes as support for selective “turn-off” determination of rutin with LOD of 30 ng mL−1 (Li et al., 2019). However, despite these advances, few sensors can ensure sufficient sensitivity and specificity to meet the requirements of rutin quantification. The development of highly sensitive sensors for rapid detection of rutin is therefore of particular importance.

Recently, the dual-mode detection has gained more attention as proved to be more efficient than single-mode detection. Dual-mode assays have been shown to be more sensitive and efficient. Besides, the individual advantages of each method used in the dual-mode detection, which provides a double check readout approach. Therefore, a more reliable and accurate quantitative analysis, especially in complicated samples, can be performed by this approach (Zhang et al., 2019; Zeng et al., 2018). Until now, several dual-mode strategies have been developed such as colorimetry/fluorescence (Ali et al., 2019), fluorescence/surface-enhanced Raman scattering (Wang et al., 2012b), and fluorescence/electrochemical (Chen et al., 2021) dual-mode detections.

Carbon dots (C-dots) have attracted particular attention nowadays owing to their simple synthesis, excellent optical properties, water-solubility, good biocompatibility, and stable chemical properties (Mahmoud et al., 2019, 2020; Alqarni et al., 2021). The main disadvantage of undoped C-dots is their low quantum yields, which can be improved by doping with heteroatoms such as boron (B), sulfur (S), phosphorous (P), and nitrogen (N) (Bian et al., 2018). Heteroatoms can effectively tune the intrinsic characters of C-dots such as electronic properties, surface chemical reactions, optical properties (Atchudan et al., 2018), and give them ideal electrochemical properties (Wohlgemuth et al., 2012). Heteroatoms doped C-dots are widely used in many fields such as sensing (Shuzhen et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2020; Mahmoud et al., 2019) and bioimaging (Atchudan et al., 2018).

Herein, new nitrogen and sulfur co-doped bright blue fluorescent C-dots (N, S@C-dots) were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method from molasses, thioacetamide, and glycine. The as-synthesized N, S@C-dots displayed some advantages such as high quantum yield, excellent water solubility, good stability, high sensitivity, satisfactory selectivity, and excellent electro-catalytic activity towards rutin detection. The N, S@C-dots and N, S@C-dots modified glassy carbon electrode were used for bimodal fluorometric and electrochemical detection of rutin, respectively. It is noteworthy to mention that it is the first report to utilize N, S@C-dots as a dual-mode sensor for the fluorometric and electrochemical detection.

Rutin hydrate (≥94 %), methionine (≥97 %), glycine (≥ 99 %), dopamine ((≥97 %), uric acid (≥97 %), thiamine (≥97 %), riboflavin (≥98 %), glutathione (≥98 %), lysine (≥97 %), cysteine (≥ 98 %), tryptophan (98 %), and bovine serum albumin (≥96 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Ascorbic acid (≥98 %), glucose (≥99 %), and sucrose (99 %) were purchased from Merck. Boric acid, phosphoric acid, acetic acid, citric acid, oxalic acid, sodium hydroxide, and hydrochloric acid were purchased from El-Nasser for chemicals, Cairo, Egypt. Double distilled water (DDW) was used throughout the study. Ruta C 60® tablets were purchased from local markets that contain 60 mg rutin and 160 mg ascorbic acid.

Cyclic voltammetric (CV) and differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) experiments were performed with Princeton VersaSTAT MC (VersaSTAT 3, Model RE-1, Princeton Applied Research, AMETEK, USA), using a conventional three-electrode system, which consisted of a platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode, Ag/AgCl, KCl as the reference electrode, and a bare/ modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) working electrode in a solution containing 5.0 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. Transmission electron microscope images were captured on TEM Assembly Parts Power of JEOL JEM-100CX II-unit tungsten EM filament 120 kV assisted by high-quality imaging camera conducted with Digital Micrograph software system of Gatan Erlangshen ES500W (USA); the drop sample was outfitted, dispersed onto a grid of carbon-coated copper, and dried under ordinary ambient conditions. Photoluminescence spectra were measured on a SCINCO FluoroMate (FS-2, Korea) spectrofluorometer. UV–vis spectra of N, S@C-dots were recorded by Shimadzu 1601PC UV–vis, Scanning Spectrophotometer. Nicolet™ iS™10 spectrometer was adapted to record Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in the 400–4000 cm−1 wave number range via the KBr pellet procedure. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) was scanned by Philips X-ray diffractometer PW 1710 supplied with 40 kV operating applied voltage, 30 mA current, 0.06 °/min scanning rate in the 2θ range of (4–60 °), and Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å); the dynamic light scattering measurements (DLS) were scanned by Zetasizer Red badge instrument of ZEN 3600 Nano ZS model (Malvern, UK). However, the elemental composition of N, S@C-dots was determined by NEX QC + QuantEZ (50 kV X-ray tube and SDD detector) energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer. Raman spectral studies were performed using Micro-Raman spectrometer (U.K) encompassed with 514.4 nm He/Ne laser.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S@C-dots)
ce:section.ce:para: The N, S@C-dots was prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. Into a glass bottle, 0.5 g molasses, 0.45 g thioacetamide, and 0.55 g glycine were dissolved in 30 mL DDW under sonication for 10 min. The resultant solution was heated at 250 °C for 7 h. The black solid product was allowed to cool down at room temperature, dried, and stored after crushed in a light- protected container. A solution of 20 mg mL−1 was prepared by dispersing 2.0 g of the product in 100 mL DDW; then the solution was homogenized by sonication for 1 h. Finally, the solution was filtered through a 0.2 μm filter membrane and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3000 Da) for 3 days while changing the deionized water every 12 h. The synthesized N, S@C-dots was freeze-dried and was stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and electrochemical sensing of rutin
ce:section.ce:para: Stock solution of rutin (1.0 mM) was prepared by dissolving appropriate amount of rutin hydrate in DDW. For the fluorometric measurement, 0.3 mL N, S@C-dots solution (20 mg mL−1) was added to 1 mL Britton-Robinson buffer (B.R., pH 7), followed by the addition of different volumes of rutin (1.0 mM). After that, 5.0 μL of 20.0 μM BSA was thoroughly mixed with the N, S@C-dots/rutin/buffer system. Finally, the solution was completed to 4 mL with DDW. After that, the solution was measured after 2 min at λem 440 nm after excitation at 360 nm. All fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature (25 °C ± 1 °C) (Scheme 1 ). For the electrochemical measurement, 0.5 μL Nafion oil was dropped onto the surface of the polished and cleaned GCE surface followed by addition of 6.0 μL N, S@C-dots solution (2.5 mg mL−1) and then air-dried for 10 min. The modified electrode (N, S@C-dots/GCE) was immersed in a voltammetric cell containing 6.0 mL B.R. buffer (pH 6.0) and different volumes of rutin solution (Scheme 1).

For the preparation of pharmaceutical tablets, twenty tablets were accurately weighed, finely powdered, and mixed well in a glass mortar. Portions equivalent to one tablet were accurately weighed and dissolved in DDW. The contents were sonicated for 20 min to ensure complete solubility and the insoluble excipients were removed by centrifugation at 3500 rpm for 5 min. The residue was washed three times with DDW to ensure complete elution of the rutin from insoluble parts (Khashaba et al., 2017).

For the preparation of human serum, 4.0 mL of blood samples from healthy and non-smoking volunteers’ forearm veins were collected into heparinized polyethylene tubes. Then, the tubes were centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 15 min, and the collected supernatants (plasma) were stored at −80 °C until analysis. All experimental procedures and protocols were reviewed and approved by the ethics of Assiut University clinics, Assiut, Egypt. After that, the human plasma (0.5 mL) was mixed with 0.75 mL acetonitrile and subjected to centrifugation for about 25 min to remove possible interference (Mahnashi et al., 2021a).

For the preparation of orange sample as a real sample, an appropriate amount of orange fruits was chopped into small pieces and transferred to 20 mL of ethanol solution, and the immersed samples were sonicated for 45 min. Then the obtained solutions were diluted with DDW, and stock solutions were prepared.

The quantum yield of the N, S@C-dots was determined according to the following equation:
Φs =Φst (Ix/Ax) (Ast/Ist) (ηx/ηst)
Where Φ, I, A, η are the quantum yield, the corrected emission intensity at the excitation wavelength, the absorbance, the refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The subscripts “st” and “x” represent reference standard and sample. The reference standard here is quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (Φ = 54.6 %) and the sample is N, S@C-dots.

The as-synthesized N, S@C-dots were favorably fabricated by one-step hydrothermal method utilizing molasses, thioacetamide, and glycine. The formation of N, S@C-dots during the hydrothermal treatment has been considered as carbonization of precursors, which is a complicated strategy involving dehydration and polymerization steps (Titirici and Antonietti, 2010). In this synthesis, molasses provided a rich carbon source for the formation of C-dots, while thioacetamide and glycine contribute to the functional doping of sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N), respectively. The as-synthesized N, S@C-dots are readily soluble in water, well dispersible, transparent, and has high quantum yield (38.7 %). Moreover, the presence of abundant polar groups facilitates the adsorption of rutin; via hydrogen bonding; on the surface of passivated N, S@C-dots during fluorometric and electrochemical determination.

TEM image (Fig.1 a) shows well-dispersed spherical particles with an average size of 7.2 ± 1 nm (Fig.1b). Moreover, the UV/VIS and fluorescence spectra were used as good evidences for the formation of N, S@C-dots. For UV/VIS, two absorption bands were seen at 253 nm and 360 nm, which correspond to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively. While, the fluorescence spectrum of N, S@C-dots shows emission and excitation bands positioned at 440 nm and 360 nm, respectively (Fig. 1c).

The PXRD pattern of N, S@C-dots exhibits a broad diffraction peak at 23.8 ° consistent with the (002) plane of graphite, attributed to a highly disordered graphene-like structure for the N, S@C-dots (Alkahtani et al., 2021; Mahnashi et al., 2021b, c) (Fig. 1e).

Fig.1f displayed the Raman spectrum of N, S@C-dots. The band at 1356 cm−1 belongs to the d-band (sp3-hyberidized carbons), and is related to the vibrations of carbon atoms. While the band at 1598 cm−1 belongs to the G-band (sp2-hyberidized carbons), and is related to the vibration sp2 hybridized carbon atom in the two-dimensional hexagonal lattice (Liu et al., 2009). The intensity ratio of the disordered D band to the crystalline G band (ID/IG) is 0.92 for N, S@C-dots, illustrating that the structure is similar to graphite.

The EDX analysis confirmed the formation of the as-synthesized N, S@C-dots. The observed peaks in Fig. S1 A show the distinctive peaks of C, N, O, and S that are good evidence for the formation of N, S@C-dots.

To confirm the surface functionalization of N, S@C-dots, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed in two stepwise weight-losses at 148.9 and 576.6 ° C. As shown in Fig.S2B, the initial weight loss of 9.5–10.0 % at 148.9 ° C is due to evaporation of H2O molecules and other molecules attached via weak hydrogen bonding within the N, S@C-dots. Furthermore, a constant weight loss of 53.4 % until 576.6 ° C may be attributed to the decomposition of organic functional groups in N, S@C-dots.

The photoluminescence emission spectra were recorded under various excitation wavelengths. It is obvious from Fig. S2A that the emission peak was shifted upon changing the excitation wavelengths from 300−390 nm. The as-synthesized N, S@C-dots demonstrated a strong emission peak at 440 nm after excitation at 360 nm. Using quinine sulfate as a reference compound, the quantum yield was estimated to 38.7 %, which may be ascribed to the structures engendered by N- and S-doping.

The most important feature for the carbon dots is stability. The as-prepared N, S@C-dots were found to be stable over a wide range of pH at 3–12 (Fig.S2B). This stability may be due to the surface charge caused by protonation/deprotonation of the surface functional groups of N, S@C-dots (Dong et al., 2013). In addition, the fluorescence intensity remained constant when the sodium chloride (NaCl) concentration reached 1200 mM, suggesting the as-prepared N, S@C-dots possessed excellent high salt amount tolerance (Fig.S2C). Fig.S2D shows the dependence of fluorescence intensity of N, S@C-dots on the temperature where it was found that the fluorescence intensity decreased with the elevation of temperature from 25 °C to 100 °C. Given this temperature range is larger than the physiological temperature, N, S@C-dots have promising applications in vivo temperature sensing.

To evaluate the electro-catalytic activity of N, S@C-dots modified glassy carbon electrode (N, S@C-dots/GCE), the bare and modified GCE were immersed in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− dissolved in 0.1 M KCl. Fig.S3A shows the current response of N, S@C-dots/GCE is higher than the bare GCE which may be attributed to the high surface area and good electrical conductivity of N, S@C-dots. Moreover, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) shows the semicircle part of N, S@C-dots/GCE position at lower resistance (325 Ω) than the bare GCE (657 Ω), indicating that the redox probe diffuses more slowly into bare GCE than the N, S@C-dots/GCE (Fig. S3B). These results prove that the N, S@C-dots/GCE is superior to the bare GCE in the electro-catalytic determination of electro-active species. In addition, the effective areas of bare GCE and N, S@C-dots/GCE were calculated via the Randles-Sevcik formula (Mahnashi et al., 2020; Alkahtani et al., 2020; El-Wekil et al., 2018):
Ipa = 2.69 × 105 n3/2 AD1/ 2ν1/ 2C

Ipa is the anodic peak current, n is the electrons transferred, A is the effective surface area, ν is the scan rate, and C (mol. mL−1) is the concentration of the redox probe, D is the diffusion coefficient of the redox probe (7.6 × 10-6 cm2 s−1), the effective surface areas of bare GCE and N, S@C-dots/GCE were 2.78 mm2 and 4.18 mm2, respectively (Fig.S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and electrochemical sensing mechanism of rutin
ce:section.ce:para: A series of measurements were carried out to figure out the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N, S@C-dots by rutin. Fig. S5A shows the spectral overlapping between the absorption band of rutin and the excitation/emission spectra of N, S@C-dots suggested that the quenching mechanism may be attributed to inner-filter effect (IFE) (Chen et al., 2014). Moreover, the quenching of N, S@C-dots is usually caused by static (no change in fluorescence lifetime) or dynamic (change in the fluorescence lifetime) quenching. Fig.S5B shows the average lifetimes of N, S@C-dots before and after addition of 3.0 μM rutin, which are 8.31 and 8.43 ns, respectively, suggesting a static quenching between rutin and N, S@C-dots (Huang et al., 2015). Therefore, the quenching mechanism is speculated to be static quenching and IFE.

Moreover, the electro-catalytic activity of 5.0 μM rutin was investigated at bare GCE and N, S@C-dots/GCE in the presence of B.R. buffer (pH 7.0) using cyclic voltammetry (CV) at a scan rate of 0.25 V s−1 and accumulation time of 90 s. Fig.S6A shows that the oxidation of rutin is reversible at GCE and N, S@C-dots/GCE. It is obvious that anodic (Epa) and cathodic (Epc) potentials of rutin oxidation at N, S@C-dots/GCE are lower than the bare GCE, suggesting the good electro-catalytic activity of N, S@C-dots/GCE. Moreover, the anodic (Ipa) and cathodic (Ipc) currents of rutin oxidation at N, S@C-dots/GCE are higher than the bare GCE, indicating that the N, S@C-dots/GCE has a large surface area, higher conductivity, and faster electron transfer than the bare GCE. Fig. S6 B shows the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) scan of 25.0 × 10-8 M rutin where it was found that the anodic oxidation current of rutin at N, S@C-dots/GCE is about five times than the bare GCE, suggesting the excellent performance of N, S@C-dots/GCE for rutin oxidation. It is noteworthy to mention that the abundant polar groups on the surface of N, S@C-dots facilitate the adsorption of polar rutin molecules via hydrogen bonding, enhancing the interaction between N, S@C-dots and rutin.

To attain maximum measurement sensitivity, factors affecting fluorescence intensity of N, S@C-dots after incubation with rutin were optimized. These experimental factors include pH, and incubation time. Fig.S7A shows that the fluorescence quenching was reached a maximum at pH = 7.0, and decreased at higher pH value. This may be due to a pH more than 7.0, OH groups of rutin may be broken which directly influence the interaction between aqueous N, S@C-dots and rutin (Hu et al., 2014). As a result, pH 7.0 was chosen as an optimum pH for the determination of rutin. In addition, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of N, S@C-dots was decreased with the increase of incubation time, but stabilized within 2 min (Fig.S7B). The fast interaction between N, S@C-dots and rutin may be attributed to the ultra-small size of N, S@C-dots and great solubility in the detection environment.

Under optimum conditions of measurements, the fluorescence intensity of N, S@C-dots decreased upon increasing the amounts of rutin (Fig. 3 A). A plot of F°/F vs. concentration of rutin in the range of 15.4–92.3 μM yielded a linear relationship with a determination coefficient (r2) of 0.9972. F° and F refer to the fluorescence emission of N, S@C-dots before and after the addition of rutin, respectively. The LOD (S/N = 3) and LOQ (S/N = 10) were calculated to be 0.008 and 0.02 μM, respectively. The linear regression for the quantitation of rutin can be ascribed as follows: F°/F = 1.15 + 0.055Crutin.

For the electrochemical oxidation, the DPV technique was preferred due to its sensitivity. Fig. 3B shows that the successive addition of rutin tends to enhance the anodic peak current (Ipa) in the linear range of 2.0–130.0 × 10−8 M The regression equation of the calibration plot can be written as: Ipa(μA)= -1.75 + 3.17 Crutin (r2 = 0.9991) with LOQ (S/N = 10) and LOD (S/N = 3) of 0.25 × 10−8 and 0.076 × 10−8 M, respectively.

The reproducibility of the proposed sensor was evaluated by the fluorometric and the electrochemical methods. The quenching evoked by 5.0 μM rutin on the N, S@C-dots emission intensity for three different days was monitored. It was found that the relative standard deviation % (RSD %) did not exceed 3.7 %, indicating the reliability of the sensor for rutin measurement. Moreover, the reproducibility of N, S@C-dots/GCE was investigated by measuring the response of six independent electrodes in the presence of 25 × 10−8 M rutin in B.R. buffer (pH 7.0). The RSD % was found 3.4 %, suggesting good reproducibility of the modified sensor for measurement of rutin.
The anodic current response remained 94.8 % of its initial value after storing N, S@C-dots/GCE at room temperature for about 17 days, reflecting the storage stability of the N, S@C-dots/GCE.

The selectivity is an important parameter to evaluate the applicability of the analytical sensor. Thus, the effect of interfering species (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, SO4 2-, citric acid, oxalic acid, glucose, sucrose, ascorbic acid, tryptophan, cysteine, lysine, glutathione, riboflavin, thiamine, uric acid, dopamine, glycine, methionine, luteolin, apigenin, myricetin, quercetin, and kaempferol on the quenching of N, S@C-dots emission and the electrochemical oxidation of rutin was studied and presented in Fig.4 . To examine the effect of interfering species, 50.0 μM of interfering species was tested under the same conditions of rutin measurement. It is obvious that only rutin can quench the fluorescence of N, S@C-dots, and the other interfering substances had no measurable effect. It is noteworthy to mention that other flavonols such as luteolin, apigenin, myricetin, quercetin, and kaempferol can quench the emission of N, S@C-dots. But, in the presence of bovine serum albumin (BSA), these flavanols (5 times more than rutin) showed little quenching effect on the emission intensity of the as-synthesized N, S@C-dots due to the masking of their effects by BSA. On contrast, the introducing of a bulky oligosaccharide substituent on rutin makes its binding ability with BSA almost five times weaker than other flavonols. As a result, rutin can remain its quenching effect toward N, S@C-dots in the solution containing BSA (Fig.5 ). From Fig.5 A, it can be concluded that rutin and quercetin (an example of interfering flavonoids) have absorption bands in the region of 380−450 nm without BSA. These similar absorption bands may be due to the similar structure of both flavonoids. When BSA coexisted in the solution, the absorption intensity of quercetin was dropped dramatically while the absorption intensity of rutin was decreased slightly (Fig. 5B). It was reported that quercetin binds to subdomain IIA of albumin (Boulton et al., 1998; Zsila et al., 2003). Moreover, the quenching effect of rutin/quercetin on the N, S@C-dots in absence and presence of BSA was studied. As shown in Fig. 5C, after addition a small amount of rutin and quercetin on N, S@C-dots, the decrease in the fluorescence intensity is almost coincidence. After addition of BSA, the quenching effect of quercetin was inhibited, while that by rutin nearly remained unchanged (Fig. 5D). For the electrochemical oxidation, no obvious oxidation peak was seen for the other flavonols (50.0 μM) referring to the good selectivity of the N, S@C-dots/GCE for measurement of rutin in the presence of other flavonols.

To evaluate the feasibility of the proposed sensor, rutin tablets, human serum, and orange fruits were selected as testing samples. The fluorometric and the electrochemical sensors were prepared and used to analyze rutin according to the experimental procedure, and a standard addition method was used to obtain recovery of the analysis. Tables 1 and 2
show that the obtained results were satisfactory, and the recovery % was in the range of 97.4–106.8 % and RSDs % not more than 4%. Moreover, the proposed methods were compared with reported fluorometric and electrochemical methods ((Li et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2017) to assess the accuracy of the results. The results of the reported method indicated the good performance of the proposed methods for the determination of rutin in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with other reported fluorometric and electrochemical sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed fluorometric and electrochemical sensor for the determination of rutin was compared with other reported sensors in Table 3 . As summarized in this Table, the as-synthesized sensor is particularly advantageous owing to its simplicity and fast nature. Moreover, wide linear ranges, cost-effectiveness, and low detection limits are the main advantages of the as-fabricated sensor.

A bimodal detection using a new N, S@C-dots was developed for a sensitive, reliable, and selective detection of rutin. The determination based on quenching the fluorescence emission of N, S@C-dots using rutin and the oxidation of rutin at N, S@C-dots modified GCE. As compared to other reported methods, the proposed fluorescence and electrochemical methods of rutin have the advantages of not only achieving wider linear ranges but also achieving lower detection limits (LODs). This bimodal testing platform is environmentally eco-friendly, low-cost, and provides low LODs for rutin detection. It provides a promising platform for the detection of rutin in complicated real samples with good recovery % and acceptable RSDs %.

Dr. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization; Data curation; Formal analysis; Investigation; Methodology; Software; Supervision; Validation; Visualization; Writing - original draft; Writing - review & editing. Prof. Dr. Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Investigation; Software; Validation; Visualization; review & editing. Dr. Mater H. Mahnashi: Resources; review & editing, funding. Dr. Adel Al Fatease: Funding acquisition; Project administration; Resources; review & editing. Dr. Mahmoud A.H. Mostafa: Software; Supervision; Validation; Visualization; Writing. Dr. Ramadan Ali: Investigation; Software; Validation; Visualization; review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfca.2022.104428.
